content.full_text:
Opinion Request Meeting Participants                    March 14, 1980


John Giansello


     The California State Employees Association is a sponsoring orga-
nization within the meaning of 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18419.

     Members pay dues to CSEA. Fifty cents per member is earmarked in
advance for political purposes and transferred by VSEA to its political
action committee. In some years this earmarking produces a surplus for
the political action committee which it carries over for political
purposes in succeeding years.

     A certain portion of the dues money not allocated for political
purposes is rebated to CSEA's local chapters for general, non-political
purposes.

     CSEA's political action committee now proposes the following:

     (1) To solicit members directly for contributions to the
         political action committee.
     (2) To ask the local chapters to contribute a portion of
         rebated funds to the political action committee.

     In addition, the CSEA board in 1979 voted to authorize the turn-
over of $84,000 of non-earmarked 1979 funds to the political action
committee for the purpose of opposing Proposition 9. The transaction
authorized by this action was not accomplished until 1980. In a
previous year (1978) the political action committee had returned
$75,000 of earmarked funds to the CSEA general fund for use for non-
political purposes.

     (A) Assuming that CSEA, its local chapters and its political
action committee report in accordance with 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18419,
which of the above actions must be taken into consideration to
determine which contributors must be itemized on the political action
committee's reports?

     (B) If the amount of rebated, non-earmarked funds is to be
considered, is it sufficient to apportion the amount rebated from a
particular chapter against the number of members of that chapter?
(The total amount of dues paid CSEA by each member is determined by
his or her income. Therefore, the portion of rebated funds actually
attributable to each member will vary from one member to another.)

Opinion Request Meeting Participants    -2-                March 14, 1980


     (C) If the amount of the CSEA board turnover is to be considered
- on the grounds that there is a sufficient history to indicate to the
membership that the 50¢ per month per member earmarking is not neces-
sarily all of the dues money that will be spent for political purposes
in a particular year - is it sufficient to apportion the total amount
of the turnover against the total membership of CSEA? (The same
concern about variable dues payments as expressed in (B) applies here.)

     (D) If the reasonable expectations of the membership afe affected
by the $84,000 turnover of non-political funds for political purposes,
what affect if any does the return in a prior year of a carry-over
political surplus to the non-political fund have?

     (E) WHAT affect if any on these itemization problems does our
recordkeeping regulation, 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18401, have when it says
that the accounts required to be kept by treasurers need not contain
detail as to contributions received of less than $25 other than the
date and total amount of such contributions?

     If,ian addition to all of the other activity described above, a
local CSEA Chapter raises $500 in additional contributions directly
from its members to be spent directly by the local chapter oe that by
virtue of activities independent of the sponsoring organization the
local chapter itself becomes a recipient committee, does 2 Cal. Admin.
Code §18419(d) mean that the local chapter need not report these con-
tributions regardless of amount?  Note that by virtue of its role in
the CSEA scheme the local chapter is an "intermediate unit." 18419(d)
items be imply that such an intermediate unit may alse become a com-
mittee by reason of "other political activities." Because it an
intermediate unit whose name is not substantially different from that
of the sponsoring organization, it need not be listed as an inter-
mediary on the parent's political action committee reports by
18419(b)(4). Furthermore, with respect to contributions raised and
spent directly by the local chapter, the latter is not in fact an
intermediary. Yet 18419(d) says that an intermediate unit which is a
committee by virtue of other political activities need not disclose
"member contributions" if its PAC follows the procedures set forth in
18419(b)(4) (which here produce no reporting).

     Is the answer that the restriction of "member contribution" by
18419(d) (maybe [illegible]ect)xxpectief [illegible]xxenintermediaterapparticipation-
intermediate unit earmarked for use by the sponsor's political action
committee together with the nondisclosure language of 18419(d) menas
that funds raised and spent by an intermediate unit independently of its
parent or the parent's political action committee are governed by the
same rules applicable to other committees. In other words, if the
intermediate unit is a committee by virtue of acts activity conducted
independently of the parent and the parent's political action com-
mittee, the intermediate unit must itemize all contributions and
expenditures of $100 or more with respect to which it acts indepen-
dently?

sections.question: The document contains multiple related questions (A-E plus a final complex question) regarding: (A) which actions must be considered to determine which contributors must be itemized on the political action committee's reports; (B) whether it is sufficient to apportion rebated, non-earmarked funds from a particular chapter against the number of members of that chapter; (C) whether it is sufficient to apportion the total amount of the CSEA board turnover against the total membership of CSEA; (D) what effect the return in a prior year of a carry-over political surplus to the non-political fund has on reasonable expectations of the membership; (E) what effect the recordkeeping regulation regarding contributions of less than $25 has on itemization problems; and whether a local CSEA Chapter that raises $500 in additional contributions directly from its members need not report these contributions, and whether funds raised and spent by an intermediate unit independently of its parent are governed by the same rules applicable to other committees.
sections.question_synthetic: The California State Employees Association seeks clarification on how to properly itemize contributor reports for its political action committee given various fund transfers between its general fund and political action committee, including earmarked dues, rebated funds, and transfers of non-political funds, and how these transactions affect reporting obligations and the treatment of contributions raised by local chapters independently of the parent organization.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document appears to be an incoming request or opinion request record rather than an FPPC response letter. No conclusion or answer section is present. The document contains only the detailed questions posed by the requestor. Marked as advice_letter per parsing notes but actual FPPC response/conclusion is not included in this document. The is_fppc_response is set to true per document_type designation, but this should be verified as the document may be incomplete or an incoming request rather than a full advice letter response.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The document contains multiple related questions (A-E plus a final complex question) regarding: (A) which actions must be considered to determine which contributors must be itemized on the political action committee's reports; (B) whether it is sufficient to apportion rebated, non-earmarked funds from a particular chapter against the number of members of that chapter; (C) whether it is sufficient to apportion the total amount of the CSEA board turnover against the total membership of CSEA; (D) what effect the return in a prior year of a carry-over political surplus to the non-political fund has on reasonable expectations of the membership; (E) what effect the recordkeeping regulation regarding contributions of less than $25 has on itemization problems; and whether a local CSEA Chapter that raises $500 in additional contributions directly from its members need not report these contributions, and whether funds raised and spent by an intermediate unit independently of its parent are governed by the same rules applicable to other committees.

embedding.summary: This is an opinion request meeting record from March 14, 1980 regarding the California State Employees Association's fund transfer practices and itemization reporting requirements under California political practice regulations, with detailed questions about contributor disclosure obligations for various types of fund transfers and local chapter activities.
